title: India’s 2025–26 Sugar Super-Cycle sees a Record Production!!
excerpt:
As of December 31, 2025, India’s sugar industry has demonstrated a remarkably robust start to
the 2025–26 marketing season, signaling a definitive recovery from the previous year's supply
constraints. According to the latest data from the National Federation of Cooperative Sugar
Factories (NFCSF), national sugar production reached 118.30 lakh metric tonnes (LMT) during the
first three months of the season (October–December).
body:
As of December 31, 2025, India’s sugar industry has demonstrated a remarkably robust start to
the 2025–26 marketing season, signaling a definitive recovery from the previous year's supply
constraints. According to the latest data from the National Federation of Cooperative Sugar
Factories (NFCSF), national sugar production reached 118.30 lakh metric tonnes (LMT) during the
first three months of the season (October–December). This represents a significant 23.4%
increase compared to the 95.60 LMT produced during the same period in 2024, underscoring a
season of high operational efficiency and favorable agricultural conditions.
This surge in production is primarily attributed to a sharp increase in sugarcane crushing and
improved recovery rates. By the end of December, approximately 499 sugar mills were
operational across the country, having crushed nearly 1,340 lakh tonnes of sugarcane. This is a
substantial leap from the 1,101 LMT crushed in the previous year. Furthermore, the average
sugar recovery rate—a key metric of industrial efficiency—improved to 8.83%, up from 8.69% in
the prior season. These gains are largely the result of a favorable 2024 monsoon, which ensured
healthy crop yields and high sucrose content in the cane.
State-wise performance highlights Maharashtra as the standout leader in this growth phase. The
state witnessed a dramatic 63% rise in output, producing 48.7 LMT of sugar compared to 29.9
LMT in the corresponding period last year. Uttar Pradesh, India’s largest sugar producer, also
maintained a steady upward trajectory with 35.6 LMT, while Karnataka contributed 22.1 LMT.
Together, these three states continue to form the backbone of India’s sugar economy, benefiting
from expanded acreage and modern milling technologies that have optimized the start of the
crushing cycle.

excerpt:

more than the headline suggests. The policy is clear: exports of wheat flour and related products (atta, maida, sooji/semolina and
allied items under HS 1101) remain prohibited in general, but are now permitted up to 5 lakh

body:
India has quietly opened a measured export window for wheat products — and it matters far
more than the headline suggests.
The policy is clear: exports of wheat flour and related products (atta, maida, sooji/semolina and
allied items under HS 1101) remain prohibited in general, but are now permitted up to 5 lakh
metric tonnes (5 LMT) through DGFT Export Authorisation.
And the operating playbook is equally specific:
Applications are filed online on the DGFT portal.
The first window runs 21–31 January 2026; after that, applications are accepted in the last 10
days of every month
Authorisations are typically valid for six months.
Allocations are screened through a committee mechanism, with scope for re-allocation based on
actual export performance.
Why this matters right now:
1) Weak rupee = stronger realisation.
Even with steady global prices, a softer rupee improves INR revenue per tonne — a rare tailwind
for Indian processors and exporters.
2) Surplus comfort = export space.
When domestic supply is comfortable and stocks feel heavy, value-added exports help ease the
pressure without reopening raw wheat exports.
3) Calibrated policy, not chaos.
A quota plus authorisation route allows control and monitoring while still enabling industry to
serve strategic markets.

body:
As of January 17, 2026 (latest close: Jan 16), spot silver is hovering around $90 per ounce, after
briefly printing above $93 this week. The positive outlook can drive it beyond $100 in the short
term and $150 beyond 12 months. But corrections in silver can be brutal.
Put the move in context. For most of the early 2020s, silver largely chopped sideways: it ended
2021–2023 around $23–$24/oz, and finished 2024 near $29/oz. Then came the break-out: silver
ended 2025 around $72/oz, and the LBMA described 2025 as a year that delivered a ~145% gain.
Why the surge? Three forces are reinforcing each other:
Macro tailwinds — softer inflation prints, rising rate-cut expectations, and a weaker USD are
lifting precious metals.
Geopolitics and safe-haven flows — investors are treating silver as a core macro asset, not a
tactical trade.
Real-world tightness + industrial pull — unlike gold, silver is consumed in the economy
(electronics, solar, electrification). Persistent structural supply deficits amplify every demand
shock.

projected a 2026 average around $68/oz, while warning prices can be “unsustainable” and swing

sharply even inside a bull market.

title: GSP Suspension by the European Union: More Impact Than Claimed, But Agri-Food Escapes the Trigger!!
excerpt:
EU “GSP suspension” headlines are creating unnecessary anxiety in India’s agri & food export
community. The reality is more nuanced—and (for agriculture) far less dramatic than the
headlines suggest.
body:
EU “GSP suspension” headlines are creating unnecessary anxiety in India’s agri & food export
community. The reality is more nuanced—and (for agriculture) far less dramatic than the
headlines suggest. This is closer to rules-based ‘graduation’ than a political sanction.
What actually changed (and why):
From 1 Jan 2026 to 31 Dec 2028, the EU will suspend GSP tariff preferences for India in a countryspecific list of “excluded sections” because imports in those sections crossed EU “graduation”
thresholds for three consecutive years (a rules-based trigger under the EU GSP framework).
Why agri & food is largely insulated (for now):
The EU’s published excluded-sections list for India is concentrated in non-agricultural groups—
minerals, chemicals, plastics/rubber, textiles, metals, machinery and transport equipment. No
agriculture/food sections appear in India’s 2026–28 exclusion list, so there is no section-wide GSP
preference withdrawal hitting HS Chapters 1–24 via this mechanism.
That matters because India’s agri exports are a meaningful macro lever, and the EU is a qualitysensitive, standards-intensive destination. In 2024, EU agri-food imports from India were €3.97
bn, up ~20% YoY; key buckets included coffee/tea/cocoa/spices (€0.95 bn), oils/fats and “nonedible technical use” items.
So why do impact estimates diverge so sharply?
The Commerce Ministry is highlighting that only ~2.66% of India’s exports to the EU lose GSP
benefits.

title: USD/INR at 97 Will Be a Policy Failure — Unless India Corrects Course Now!!
excerpt:
Let’s be clear and unsentimental. If USD/INR drifts toward 97 by end of 2026, which looks a reality today, it will not be an
inevitable outcome of a strong dollar.
body:
Let’s be clear and unsentimental.
If USD/INR drifts toward 97 by end of 2026, which looks a reality today, it will not be an
inevitable outcome of a strong dollar. It will be the result of avoidable Indian policy failures—
and prolonged complacency.
This is not a currency crisis. It is a governance and execution deficit.
What Has Gone Wrong
India’s macro story looks strong on the surface, but beneath it lies a misalignment between trade
policy, industrial strategy, and currency management.
- The trade deficit is no longer cyclical; it is structural.
- Import dependence on energy, edible oils, electronics, capital goods, and fertilizers remains
unchecked.
- Export growth relies disproportionately on services, while manufacturing exports lack scale
and consistency.
- Policy flip-flops have eroded long-term trade confidence.
A weaker rupee is now being quietly tolerated as a substitute for competitiveness, instead of
fixing the competitiveness itself.
Why RBI Alone Cannot Fix This
The central bank can smooth volatility, not solve structural leaks. FX reserves are being used
defensively because policy coordination is absent. Currency stability cannot rest indefinitely on
intervention while fiscal, trade, and industrial policies pull in different directions.
If USD/INR reaches 97, it signals not panic—but policy exhaustion.

title: USD Lost Over 10% Against the Euro Last Year. Will 2026 Repeat the Script?
excerpt:
Last year marked a clear turning point in global currency markets. The US dollar lost more than
10% against the euro, a sharp reversal from its earlier dominance.
body:
Last year marked a clear turning point in global currency markets. The US dollar lost more than
10% against the euro, a sharp reversal from its earlier dominance. This was not a flash move
driven by panic, but a slow, persistent repricing of macro realities.
The big question now is simple: was that a one-off adjustment, or the start of a longer trend that
could repeat in 2026?
Why the Dollar Fell Last Year
The dollar’s weakness was fundamentally policy-driven, not crisis-driven.
First, the interest-rate advantage of the US narrowed sharply. Markets began pricing in a Federal
Reserve that had reached the peak of its tightening cycle and was shifting toward easing. As yield
differentials compressed, the incentive to park capital in dollar assets diminished.
Second, Europe surprised on resilience. Growth did not collapse as feared, energy shock risks
faded, and capital began flowing back into euro-denominated assets. The euro benefited not from
exceptional strength, but from expectations becoming less pessimistic.
Third, the dollar faced structural fatigue. Large fiscal deficits, rising debt issuance, and growing
political uncertainty weakened the “one-way long USD” trade that had dominated earlier years.
The result was a broad, orderly depreciation—enough to erase more than a decade of perceived
dollar invincibility.
Will 2026 See a Repeat?
A carbon copy decline of another 10% is possible, but not guaranteed.
The conditions that weakened the dollar are still present, but less powerful. The Fed may
continue easing, but the pace is expected to be gradual. The ECB is also cutting rates, which limits
how much further interest-rate divergence can move against the dollar.
author: Know Your Commodity

title: RMB vs USD: Managed Stability in a World of Currency Volatility, 7 +/-2%!!
excerpt:
The RMB–USD relationship is fundamentally different from most major currency pairs. The
Chinese yuan is not a fully market-determined currency.
body:
The RMB–USD relationship is fundamentally different from most major currency pairs. The
Chinese yuan is not a fully market-determined currency. It is a strategically managed instrument,
reflecting China’s economic priorities, capital controls, and geopolitical posture rather than pure
price discovery. What is important to understand is that they do have muscle to manage.
How RMB Has Behaved Against USD
Over the past year, the RMB has remained under appreciatory pressure against the US dollar, but
the move has been orderly and capped. While the dollar weakened sharply against the euro and
some other currencies, it did not against the yuan. This divergence is intentional.
China has allowed gradual weakening of dollar, not a free fall. The message is clear:
Support exports and manufacturing competitiveness.
Avoid capital flight.
Prevent financial instability.
The People’s Bank of China (PBoC) has repeatedly intervened—through fixing rates, liquidity
tools, and moral suasion—to keep USD/CNY within a politically and economically acceptable
band of 7 +/- 3.5% for nearly a decade.
Why RMB Move with Restrictions
Three structural constraints define RMB behavior:

China restricts free capital movement. This limits speculative attacks but also suppresses natural

India’s 2025–26 supply position is comfortable, not tight.

global demand shock hiding behind these price moves.

and consumer goods. Our growth potential has officially been revised upward to 7.0%.

In a world of "friend-shoring" and reciprocal tariffs, India hit record exports of $825.3 billion in
FY25. While merchandise trade faces headwinds, our services surplus and record remittances

Budget 2026: What Indian Agriculture Needs — With Numbers That Matter!!
As we head into Budget 2026, Indian agriculture needs more than comforting words. It needs
capital reallocation, sharper priorities, and system-building.
Below is a policy-forward set of realistic expectations for this year.

1️⃣ PM-KISAN: From Safety Net to Transition Tool
Allocation in Budget 2025-26: ₹63,500 crore

Expectation for Budget 2026: ₹75,000–90,000 crore (if annual support moves from ₹6,000 to
₹9,000 or is better targeted)

💡Any increase could be paired with productivity-linked interventions so income support
enables transition, not dependence.
2️⃣ Pulses & Oilseeds: Strategic Crops Need Strategic Money
Current combined mission-style allocations (2025-26): ~₹3,500 crore
Expectation for 2026: ₹6,000 crore with sharper design

💡Fund the full chain—seeds, assured procurement, decentralised processing, and export
enablement.
3️⃣ Food Subsidy: Stabilise, Don’t Expand
Allocation in Budget 2025-26: ~₹2.03 lakh crore

Expectation for 2026: Broadly flat or marginal increase (₹2.05–2.10 lakh crore)

💡Redirect incremental funds toward storage modernisation, logistics, and buffer management
efficiency. Inflation control depends on physical capacity, not just policy switches.
4️⃣ Fertiliser Subsidy: Reform the Architecture
Allocation in Budget 2025-26: ~₹1.68 lakh crore
Expectation for 2026: ₹1.70 lakh crore.

title: Trade Deficit Reality Check: India’s 2024–25 Scorecard and FY 2025– 26 (Apr–Dec) Signals
excerpt:
_*The Deficit Headline (goods + services)*_
FY 2024–25 (Apr 2024–Mar 2025): total exports US$820.9 bn vs imports US$915.2 bn → net trade
gap ~US$94.3 bn. This “contained” deficit is largely a services story, not a merchandise

body:
_*The Deficit Headline (goods + services)*_
FY 2024–25 (Apr 2024–Mar 2025): total exports US$820.9 bn vs imports US$915.2 bn → net trade
gap ~US$94.3 bn. This “contained” deficit is largely a services story, not a merchandise
turnaround.
_*Merchandise*_
FY 2024–25 goods exports were US$437.4 bn against imports of US$720.2 bn, leaving a
merchandise deficit of US$282.8 bn. The gap is structurally driven by energy, bullion and critical
intermediates; exports are improving in engineering, electronics and pharma, but not yet at the
scale to offset import-intensity in a high-growth economy.
_*Services*_
Implied FY 2024–25 services exports were ~US$383.5 bn and services imports ~US$195.0 bn,
producing a surplus near US$188.6 bn—the shock absorber that keeps the overall deficit from
tracking the merchandise gap.
_*Agriculture & Food*_
Agri exports in FY 2024–25 were about US$51.9 bn (notably rice ~US$12.5 bn and
meat/dairy/poultry ~US$5.1 bn). Yet edible oils and some protein/pulse imports remain the “leak”
that prevents agriculture from becoming a stronger net FX earner at scale.
_*India–US Trade (merchandise)*_
FY 2024–25 India–US goods trade was about US$132.2 bn: exports to the US ~US$86.5 bn, imports
~US$45.7 bn, surplus ~US$40.8 bn. In FY 2025–26 H1, exports to the US were ~US$25.5 bn (Apr–
Jun 2025) and ~US$20.3 bn (Jul–Sep 2025), showing quarter-to-quarter cooling even as the US
stayed the top destination.

body:
Feed Inflation Is a Policy Choice: Make the Soyabean meal Duty-Free (Smartly)!!
If India can allow duty-free Maize DDGS and is also opening space for soybean oil imports, then
keeping soyameal behind a high non-tariff wall is more emotional than economics.
Here’s the simple truth: India’s fastest-growing “protein consumers” are not humans — they are
chickens and fish. Poultry and aquaculture need consistent, affordable, high-quality protein.
When protein prices spike, the shock travels straight into egg, chicken and fish prices, and
affordability to consumers weakens.
So why soyameal matters:
1) Soyameal is the backbone protein of feed.
It is the most widely used plant protein for balanced rations across poultry and aqua. When
soyameal becomes expensive or volatile, feed formulators are forced to compromise—either on
nutrition or on cost. Neither is good for farmers, integrators, nor consumers.
2) The GMO argument is already blurred by DDGS.
If DDGS is coming in (and it will largely be GMO-linked in origin), then drawing a “GMO purity
line” only on soyameal becomes inconsistent. Policy must be coherent: either define a clear,
science-based framework for imports, or stop pretending one product is fundamentally different
from the other.
3) The soybean sector impact is already larger via oil.
Let’s be honest: if cheap soybean oil imports increase, crushing economics get stressed anyway.
In that context, blocking soyameal doesn’t “save” soybean farmers—it simply changes where the
pressure shows up. A tighter, more rational approach is to stabilise feed protein while designing
safeguards for domestic crushers and farmers.

India’s 2.5 MMT Wheat Push: Testing Global Price Floors or India’s Export Parity?
India’s decision to inject an additional 2.5 million metric tonnes of wheat into global markets
comes at a time when international grain prices are subdued—but not collapsing. The latest
signals from benchmark exchanges show a far more nuanced reality than a simple bearish
narrative.
Paris Euronext milling wheat is currently trading along a forward curve of roughly €190–215 per
tonne, translating to about $205–232 per metric tonne. This indicates that Europe is holding a
firm structural floor near the $220 range, supported by tighter farmer selling, weather risks, and
cautious global stock rebuilding. Not to forget European Premium.
In contrast, CBOT wheat continues to hover closer to $195–205 per tonne equivalent, keeping
Chicago at a discount of roughly $20–30 per tonne to European values. This spread is critical—it
defines the real competitive corridor for exporters worldwide.
Will India Push Prices Lower?
In absolute global supply terms, 2.5 MMT is modest. The world wheat trade exceeds 200 MMT
annually, meaning India’s additional volume is sentiment-shaping rather than market-breaking.
With Euronext still above $220 and CBOT near $200, India’s exports are more likely to cap rallies
than trigger a fresh decline. Prices today are already near cyclical lows, and incremental supply
mainly reinforces stability, not collapse.
The Real Question: Can India Achieve Export Parity?
The sharper implication lies in competitiveness, not price direction.
To succeed, Indian wheat must fit within a tight global parity band of roughly $200–230 per
tonne. Yet exporters face structural pressures:
MSP-linked procurement costs

body:
Good Optics, Tough Execution: The Real Test of India’s Wheat & Sugar Export Window!!
Policy announcements often shape sentiment faster than they shape trade. India’s recent easing
of export availability for wheat, wheat products, and sugar has certainly generated domestic
optimism and headline excitement. Yet, early market behaviour suggests a more cautious global
response — raising an important question: will this translate into meaningful export execution,
or remain strong optics with limited substance?
Consider sugar first. The export quota released nearly a quarter ago has seen less than a quarter
actually used so far. This gap between permission and performance reflects the realities of global
parity, freight economics, and buyer preferences. A similar pattern is emerging in wheat
products — especially flour — where demand response has been at best lukewarm, despite
policy easing.
More telling is the muted reaction from traditional wheat-importing regions in the Middle East
and Africa. Global traders in these corridors — who typically respond quickly to any new origin
entering the market — have not shown significant excitement. That signals that price
competitiveness and timing, not merely availability, will determine whether Indian cargoes
move.
Timing becomes even more critical when viewed against futures market dynamics. Once March
contract covering concludes, seasonal and sentiment-driven softening in global wheat prices
often follows. If Indian wheat reaches export channels during this weakening phase, margin
pressure could intensify. Simultaneously, this is also the period when the Indian government reenters procurement markets, potentially pushing domestic prices upward — further tightening
export parity.

body:
A New Era for Afro-Mandarin Trade: China Opens Doors to 53 African Nations with Zero Tariffs!!
In a massive move for global trade dynamics, China has officially announced the implementation
of zero-tariff treatment for imports from 53 African countries, effective May 1, 2026.
This isn't just a minor policy adjustment; it’s a comprehensive opening of the world's secondlargest economy to nearly an entire continent.
Here are the verified facts:

✅ The Policy: China will exempt imports from virtually all African nations having diplomatic
relations with Beijing from tariffs.
✅ The Scope: The zero-tariff treatment covers 100% of tariff lines. This is a significant upgrade
from previous schemes that only covered specific goods or LDCs (Least Developed Countries).
✅ The Timeline: The policy comes into full force on May 1, 2026, as confirmed by Chinese state
media and announcements surrounding the recent African Union summit interactions.
✅ The Exception: The policy applies to 53 nations. The only African nation excluded is Eswatini,
which does not hold diplomatic relations with the People's Republic of China.
✅ The Context: This move delivers on pledges made during the 2024 Forum on China-Africa
Cooperation (FOCAC) summit in Beijing and the Changsha Declaration in June 2025.
Why This Matters:
1. Rebalancing Trade Flows

⚖️

Historically, trade between China and Africa has been heavily skewed. China exports finished
goods and technology, while Africa exports raw materials (oil, minerals). By eliminating tariffs
on all lines, China is incentivizing the import of non-resource goods—think agriculture,
processed foods, and light manufacturing—potentially helping African nations move up the
value chain.

body:
Ocean Freight 2026: Navigating Overcapacity, Geopolitics, and the New Economics of Sea Trade!!
The ocean freight market in 2026 is defined less by dramatic shocks and more by subtle
structural pressures that are quietly reshaping global shipping economics. Beneath seemingly
stable trade flows lie a persistent imbalance between vessel supply and cargo demand—an
imbalance created by aggressive fleet expansion during the pandemic boom and now unfolding
as structural overcapacity across major trade lanes.
While fleet capacity continues to expand, demand growth remains modest, creating downward
pressure on freight rates and forcing carriers to abandon passive pricing strategies. Instead,
shipping lines are actively managing supply through blank sailings, slower vessel speeds, and
selective deployment of tonnage to defend rate floors and maintain utilization. This shift marks a
fundamental transformation in how ocean freight profitability is sustained in a slower-growth
world.
Trade lane dynamics further complicate the picture. Diversions caused by geopolitical tensions
and security disruptions are absorbing meaningful portions of global capacity, preventing a full
collapse in rates despite weak cargo volumes. At the same time, changing consumption patterns
and nearshoring trends are reducing long-haul tonne-miles while increasing the importance of
regional maritime corridors—quietly redrawing the geography of sea trade.
Regional contrasts are becoming sharper. Transpacific routes show stabilization in market share
but softer volumes, while Asia-Europe lanes remain highly sensitive to security developments
that could suddenly release trapped capacity and trigger rapid rate corrections. In Latin America,
agricultural export surges are driving strong bulk demand even as inland bottlenecks and port
congestion raise total logistics costs, reminding shippers that maritime strength does not always
translate into supply chain efficiency.

body:
Arrival Waves, Not Scarcity: Tur’s Rally Faces a Reality Check!!
India’s pulses market is approaching a moment of truth.
For months, rising prices in pigeon peas (Tur/Arhar) have been explained through narratives of
tight supply and delayed harvests. Yet a closer reading of arrivals, imports, and stock positions
suggests a different story—one where physical availability is steadily overpowering speculation.
The core numbers are difficult to ignore. India’s 2025–26 Tur balance sheet remains broadly
comfortable: production near 2.7 million tonnes, imports around 1.0 million tonnes, and opening
stocks of roughly 0.7 million tonnes together create total availability close to 4.4 million tonnes.
Against consumption of about 3.9 million tonnes, the projected carry-out of nearly 0.5 million
tonnes does not resemble a shortage market.
It reflects adequacy—perhaps even mild comfort.
The global picture reinforces this calm. World production is estimated near 4.0 million tonnes,
led by India, Myanmar, and African origins. Total supply approaches 4.8 million tonnes,
comfortably above global consumption of roughly 4.1 million tonnes, leaving meaningful ending
stocks.
Crucially, India absorbs nearly 95% of global Tur consumption, meaning recent price behaviour
cannot be attributed to an unseen surge in international demand.
Market signals within India tell a similar story.
Across Tur, Urad, Chana, Lentils, and Yellow Peas, mandi firmness has repeatedly been followed
by swift cooling—a classic signature of arrival-time speculation rather than structural scarcity.
With Myanmar exports normalising, African future supply intact, and domestic arrivals from
peninsular India building pace, prices are likely to find an anchor in physical supply. Meanwhile,
Chana and Lentils face pressure from Australian and Canadian inflows, while weak Yellow Pea
demand adds to the softer undertone across the pulses basket.

Is the Only Path Forward: E30 by 2030, Price Parity, and Ethanol Exports?
India’s ethanol programme is no longer at a stage where minor quota tweaks can solve structural
stress. The recent appeals—from sugar cooperatives seeking higher diversion quotas to grainproducing states demanding larger procurement allocations—are not competing demands. They
are warning signals.
The present allocation-driven system is stretching both sugar mills and grain ethanol producers
simultaneously. The only durable solution now is bold and clear: commit to E30 by 2030,
rationalise ethanol pricing across feedstocks, and enable ethanol exports with calibrated
incentives.
India today faces a paradox. Sugarcane production remains strong, yet sugar mills are financially
strained due to high cane prices and limited export competitiveness. At the same time, grainbased ethanol capacity has expanded aggressively, particularly in states like Bihar, where
production exceeds allocated procurement. Oil marketing companies are navigating between
feedstocks under an administered pricing framework that distorts incentives rather than
optimising economics. The result is predictable—surplus potential, idle capacity, liquidity stress,
and farmer anxiety.
Accelerating the blending roadmap to E30 by 2030 transforms this tension into opportunity.
Higher blending immediately expands structural domestic demand, absorbing additional ethanol
from both sugar and grain routes. Instead of feedstocks competing for a fixed quota, they
participate in a growing energy market. This strengthens energy security, reduces crude import
dependency, and creates stable demand visibility for farmers and processors alike.
However, higher blending alone will not suffice without price rationalisation. Current
differential pricing between sugar-based and grain-based ethanol is skewing procurement

body:
SCOTUS clips POTUS wings on “emergency tariffs”: a 6–3 rebuke to IEEPA tariff power!!
Today (Feb 20, 2026), the U.S. Supreme Court delivered a landmark 6–3 decision striking down
the core of President Donald Trump’s sweeping “global/reciprocal” tariffs that were imposed
using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) of 1977.
The Court’s message is blunt: IEEPA may be an emergency economic powers statute, but it is not
a blank cheque to rewrite tariff schedules.
Tariffs are taxes. And in the U.S. constitutional design, the primary tariff lever sits with Congress
—not the Oval Office.
What changes immediately
The broad, across-the-board tariffs justified through IEEPA are invalidated.
Tariffs imposed under other legal routes (think national-security and trade-law tools) are not
automatically touched—meaning the tariff map does not go to “zero,” it gets re-routed.
The real market story: remedies + refunds
The next battlefield is not ideology, it’s accounting. Importers will now press for duty recovery.
Estimates suggest well over $175B of tariff revenue could be at risk of refund depending on how
remedies are structured and how quickly claims move through the system.
Implications for business strategy
Policy volatility doesn’t vanish—it changes form. Future tariffs will likely shift toward statutes
with more process (investigations, findings, timelines), making them slower but potentially more
durable.
Contracting needs a legal-risk layer: tariff pass-through clauses, renegotiation triggers, and
documentation discipline will matter as much as hedging.

body:
Decoding POTUS' New 10% Tariff: A Bold Pivot Under Section 122 of the Trade Act!!
In the immediate aftermath of the U.S. Supreme Court’s Feb 20, 2026 decision curbing IEEPAbased “global/reciprocal” tariffs, Donald Trump has reached for a different lever: a temporary,
across-the-board 10% import surcharge for 150 days under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974,
over and above tariffs already in place.
The signal is clear—trade policy isn’t retreating; it’s being re-engineered inside the guardrails the
Court just drew.
Overview of Section 122
Section 122 (19 U.S.C. § 2132) was designed for moments when the U.S. faces “large and serious”
balance-of-payments deficits or other “fundamental international payments problems.” It allows
the President to impose a temporary import surcharge (up to 15%) or quotas—but only for up to
150 days unless U.S. Congress extends it. Unlike Section 301/232 pathways, it doesn’t require a
formal investigation, and it’s never been used, meaning courts have had little reason to interpret
its limits—until now.
Interpretation of the 10% Tariff Under Section 122
The administration’s theory is straightforward: persistent external imbalances justify temporary
action—without the long lead times of Section 301 investigations or Section 232 processes. The
10% level (below the 15% ceiling) functions as a stop-gap replacement while the White House
simultaneously tees up longer-horizon tools—especially new Section 301 probes—aimed at a
more targeted, more durable tariff architecture.
Implications for Trade
In the short run, a flat surcharge behaves like sand in the gears: higher landed costs, tighter
margins, and faster re-pricing across manufacturing inputs, retail inventories, and agri/food

Policy Continuity Likely in Pulses: Stability, Not Shock, Is the Government’s Current Playbook!!
As the March 31, 2026 policy horizon approaches, market chatter has intensified around possible
duty changes in the pulses basket. However, reading the current price environment, inflation
trajectory, and farmer realisation trends together suggests that policy continuity—not disruption
—is the most probable outcome in the near term.
The government today is operating in a relatively balanced zone. On one hand, farmgate prices
across key pulses remain broadly remunerative, avoiding the political and economic pressure
that typically triggers protectionist tightening. On the other, retail pulses inflation has stayed in
low single digits, removing the urgency for aggressive import liberalisation. In such a calibrated
environment, policymakers historically prefer fine-tuning over frequent reversals.
Against this backdrop, the most likely scenario is an extension of the existing policy framework
with minimal structural change:
Tur and Urad: Duty-free import window likely to continue to ensure supply comfort and prevent
volatility.
Desi Chickpeas and Lentils: The current ~10% duty structure provides a balanced cushion—
protective enough for farmers yet not inflationary for consumers.
Yellow Peas: The 30% duty regime is already moderating inflows and supporting domestic pulse
substitution dynamics; a status quo extension appears logical.
Kabuli Chickpeas: At 40% duty, the segment remains adequately shielded, and no immediate
recalibration signals are visible.
Moong: Existing import restrictions are likely to remain intact, given the government’s consistent
preference to protect domestic green gram growers.

under Section 122 of the Trade Act), sends a powerful signal: U.S. trade pressure is not easing—it

After the Supreme Court curtailed the use of IEEPA for sweeping tariffs, the administration
moved swiftly to Section 122. The initial 10% surcharge has now been ratcheted up by 50% to the
statutory ceiling of 15%, underscoring that the policy objective remains firmly intact even as the
legal pathway changes.
From a strategic lens, three critical developments emerge.

1️⃣ First, this is policy adaptation, not retreat.

By moving immediately to the maximum permissible level under Section 122, the White House is
signaling continuity of its protectionist posture. The Court may have narrowed one fast-track
route, but Washington is demonstrating it still possesses meaningful tariff leverage—albeit timebound to the 150-day window unless Congress intervenes.

2️⃣ Second, uncertainty for global trade has actually increased.

Section 122 is temporary and historically untested at this scale. A jump to 15% raises the
probability of supply-chain repricing, contract renegotiations, and accelerated sourcing shifts.
Markets now face a more complex scenario: short-duration but high-intensity tariff shocks,
potentially followed by targeted Section 301 or Section 232 actions.

The escalation to the maximum cap is aimed at preserving bargaining power with major trading

The Procurement Premium: Early Buyers Pay, Late Buyers Win!!
The first buyer in India’s wheat season pays the “procurement premium.”
That’s the irony. On paper, the world is not short on wheat. The global ending stock of 280 MMT
is historic. Availability across Russia, Ukraine, Australia, the US, and India remains broadly
comfortable, and the oversupply narrative is hard to ignore. Even when Black Sea prices feel
sticky, it is less about shortage and more about execution—freight, timing, corridors, and origin
risk.
Now place that global comfort next to India’s procurement window.
Early arrivals are not a true market. They are an MSP-anchored market. The procurement
machine opens, liquidity shifts to government buying, and spot availability for private buyers
tightens. In this phase, the first private players are competing not only with each other, but with
the strongest buyer in the room—agencies operating at MSP. So the first-to-post ends up wearing
the cost: higher basis, thinner volumes, and hurried quality decisions.
But this “tightness” is temporary. And that’s exactly why going long early in India has
diminishing returns this year.
Prices are bound to correct below MSP:
Oversupply meets post-procurement gravity. Once peak procurement passes, the market begins
to behave like a market again. Private trade returns, urgency fades, and price discovers its
natural level—often below MSP in non-procurement channels, especially where government
presence is light.
Storage is the silent bear. Lack of storage doesn’t support prices; it accelerates selling pressure. If
warehouses fill up, if mills can’t stack inventories, if movement slows, the market doesn’t reward

body:
Rice Surplus: A Balance-Sheet Risk, not a Food-Security Trophy!!
India’s rice story has been framed as proof of strength: record procurement, bulging stocks, and
“we can feed the world.” But in 2026, we need to say the quiet part out loud:
When rice stocks spill beyond buffer norms, surplus stops being security—and starts becoming a
balance-sheet liability.
Because a “surplus” is not free. It sits on the public balance sheet as:
Working capital locked for months (and sometimes years)
Interest + storage + handling costs that quietly balloon into thousands of crores
Quality deterioration risk (time is not kind to grain)
Logistics choke points that paralyse the next crop cycle
Miller distress, when the system’s cash cycle breaks and by-products don’t clear
This is the paradox: we celebrate procurement like a trophy, then wonder why godowns are
choking, mills look unviable, and export offers soften when global buyers hesitate.
Food security is not measured by “how much we can store.” It must be measured by how
efficiently we can rotate—from farmgate to consumer, from inventory to nutrition, from stock to
stability. The new operating principle: Rotate fast. Don’t hoard.
If India wants surplus without stress, policy must shift from accumulation to asset management.
What immediate intervention should look like (not slogans):
1) Create space—fast Use OMSS aggressively with realistic clearing prices. Old stock must move
before wheat pressure and before quality cost escalates.
2) Convert inventory to cash or kind. Surplus grain is a financial asset only when it can be
monetised. Scale structured channels: institutional offtake, welfare distribution efficiency, and
credible G2G/humanitarian pipelines.

Tariffs Didn’t Shrink the Deficit—They Rewired It!!
The headline number barely moved. The underlying trade architecture clearly did.
In 2025, the U.S. trade deficit stood at $901.5 billion, only marginally lower than $903–904 billion
in 2024.
At first glance, critics claim tariffs failed. But partner-level data shows a different story: the
deficit was redistributed, supply chains diversified, and U.S. leverage arguably strengthened.
Headline Stable, Structure Shifting
U.S. exports (2025): ~$3.43 trillion (+~6% YoY)
U.S. imports (2025): ~$4.33 trillion (+~5% YoY)
Goods deficit: widened to ~$1.24 trillion
Services surplus: expanded to ~$339 billion
The services cushion masked significant bilateral realignment.
Top Partner Realignment

🇨🇳 China — Strategic Compression
2025 deficit: ~$202B
2024: ~$295B
Change: ↓ ~32%
Meaning: Concentrated dependency materially reduced but capital goods flow improved.

🇲🇽 Mexico — Nearshoring Surge
2025 deficit: ~$197B

Stay Disciplined as Arrivals Build and Upside Looks Capped!!
The new desi chickpeas (chana) season has begun to unfold across key mandis in Andhra
Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, and Maharashtra, and the early signals are instructive. Prices are
currently scattered across a wide band, largely reflecting differences in moisture and lot quality
rather than any structural tightness in supply. As is typical in the early harvest window, the
market is still in the process of true price discovery.
What stands out this year is that the broader supply backdrop—both domestic and global—
appears comfortable. Early arrivals, though uneven, are expected to gather pace over the coming
weeks. At the same time, competing pulse supplies globally remain adequate, and there is no
visible trigger at present that would justify a sustained sharp rally in desi chana. This suggests
that the market is more likely to settle into a range-bound pattern rather than a strong bullish
trend through the peak arrival phase.
In this environment, procurement strategy must prioritise discipline over aggression. For fair
average quality material, the market is likely to gravitate just slightly below MSP, which remains
a natural psychological and commercial anchor. Paying significant premiums above this level in
the early arrival phase could expose buyers to mark-to-market risk as mandi inflows deepen and
quality improves.
Equally important is the focus on mid-quality, commercially workable lots rather than chasing
only top-grade material. The current crop shows variability in moisture and grain uniformity,

The Palm Oil Paradox: Navigating a Well-Supplied World!!
The palm oil market is currently a masterclass in market psychology versus structural reality. As
we move through the final week of February 2026, the Crude Palm Oil (CPO) complex is under
sustained pressure, grappling with a fundamental shift in export velocity and a cooling of the
"policy fever" that defined the market's bullish run late last year.
The Price Reality: Testing the RM 4,000 Support
The bearish momentum on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives (BMD) is no longer speculative.
Benchmark futures are aggressively testing the RM 4,000 per tonne floor—a level that served as a
launchpad in 2025 but now acts as a ceiling. This retreat from the RM 4,150 highs of early
February is driven by a stark reality: supply is catching up to demand, and the "premium" once
attributed to scarcity is evaporating.
The "Biofuel De-escalation" Factor
For months, the market was buoyed by Indonesia’s ambitious B50 biodiesel mandate. However,
recent signals from Jakarta suggesting a tactical delay in full implementation until the latter half
of 2026 have triggered a significant "policy de-risking" event.
The Scarcity Premium Fades: Without the immediate draw of millions of additional tonnes into
the domestic Indonesian energy pool, the exportable surplus is higher than analysts projected for
Q1 2026.
Energy Spillovers: The POGO (Palm Oil - Gasoil) spread has narrowed, making palm-based
biodiesel less economically attractive for discretionary blending, further weighing on the
industrial price floor.

The recent rise in the Baltic Dry Index (BDI) to approximately 2,129 points (as of February 26,
2026) reflects a significant structural shift in global grain logistics.
The current momentum is uniquely anchored by the Panamax and Supramax segments, which
are the primary workhorses for the record-breaking grain outflows from the Southern
Hemisphere.
1. The "Southern Hemisphere Engine"
The primary driver of this rally is the synchronization of record harvests across three major
Southern Hemisphere producers:
Brazil (Soybeans): A projected record harvest of 180 million metric tons (MMT) (up 5% y/y),
representing roughly 42% of global production. The front-loading of these exports in the
February–April window is creating a massive demand for tonnage in the South Atlantic.
Argentina (Wheat): A vertical recovery with a crop estimated at 27.8 MMT, nearly 50% larger
than the previous season.
Australia (Wheat): Production estimates have been revised upward to 35.6 MMT (up 8% y/y),
fueling high-frequency short and medium-haul runs to Southeast Asian markets.

The Saudi Poultry Pivot: India’s Exclusion vs. USA’s Strategic Edge!!
The Saudi Food and Drug Authority (SFDA) recently updated its regulatory framework for
poultry imports, implementing a sophisticated mix of full and partial bans. This move represents
one of the most significant updates to the Kingdom's food safety and biosecurity protocols.
1. Scope of the Restrictions
The SFDA's current measures categorize exporting nations based on the specific epidemiological
risk they pose, primarily concerning Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) and Newcastle
Disease.
Full Nationwide Ban (40 Countries): Imports of all poultry meat, table eggs, and their derivatives
are completely prohibited from 40 nations. Key countries on this list include India, the United
Kingdom, China, Germany, Egypt, and Vietnam.
Partial/Regional Ban (16 Countries): For nations with localized outbreaks, the SFDA has
implemented "zonal" restrictions rather than a blanket ban. This applies to specific provinces or
states in the United States, France, Brazil (selected regions), Poland, and Australia. This allows
trade to continue from disease-free zones within those countries.
2. The "Heat-Treatment" Exemption
Crucially, these bans are not absolute for processed goods. The SFDA provides a critical window
for trade continuity through a Heat-Treatment Exemption:
Condition: Poultry products that have undergone thermal processing sufficient to eliminate viral
pathogens (Avian Flu/Newcastle) are exempt.

body:
On February 28, 2026, the launch of Operation Epic Fury & Roaring Lion transitioned the Middle
East from a zone of "monitored tension" to a theater of active, large-scale conflict. For global
trade, this isn't just another supply chain disruption; it is the definitive end of the "efficiencyfirst" era.
We are now entering a period defined by the Geopolitical Uncertainty Premium—a structural tax
on every barrel of oil, every ton of grain, and every container moving through the Eastern
Hemisphere.
1.The Weaponization of Chokepoints
The Strait of Hormuz and the Bab el-Mandeb are no longer just transit points; they are strategic
levers.
The Logistics "Time-Tax": With the Persian Gulf and Red Sea facing high-intensity risk, the "Cape
of Good Hope" reroute will become the default for global shipping. This adds 14–20 days to
transit, effectively shrinking global vessel capacity by 15–20% overnight.
Insurance & Freight Parity: War risk premiums have decoupled from historical norms. We might
be seeing freight rates reach "Black Swan" levels, as the cost of securing a vessel will rival the
value of the cargo itself in high-risk corridors.
2.The Commodity Complex: Beyond Energy
While Brent crude is the primary indicator—possibly testing the $100 range in short term and
$130 in medium term—the "uncertainty premium" will be bleeding into the entire commodity
complex due to the compounding effect of energy costs and logistics.

excerpt:

body:
The ongoing conflict has transitioned the Middle East from a zone of "monitored tension" to a
theater of active, large-scale conflict. For global trade, this is the definitive end of the "stable" era.
We are now navigating the Hormuz Paradox: a physical blockade of the world’s most critical
energy chokepoint coupled with a structural price surge toward $130 per barrel or beyond.

🇯🇵 : The Energy Emergency

1. Japan

Japan is the most vulnerable nation to a physical blockade.
Physical Risk: It relies on the Strait for 80% of its oil and a massive share of its LNG.
Price Surge: High crude prices severely impact Japan’s trade balance, weakening the Yen further.
Buffer: Its only defense is a robust 180-day strategic petroleum reserve.
2. India

🇮🇳 : The Fiscal Breaking Point

India faces the highest macroeconomic sensitivity, where high oil directly threatens social
stability.
Physical Risk: Roughly 50% of India's crude and 54% of its LNG transit the Strait.
Reserves: With an SPR of only 74 days, India has the thinnest buffer among major importers.
Inflation: Every $10 hike adds $14 billion to India’s import bill, LNG spikes can increase urea
costs by 30-40% and straining government subsidy budget.
3. China

🇨🇳 : Strategic Diversification

China’s exposure is vast but strategically mitigated by long-term planning.
Physical Risk: It is the largest importer via the Strait (5M barrels/day).
Mitigation: A decade of diversification via pipelines from Russia and a lead in renewables
provides a unique cushion.
Leverage: China often leverages diplomatic ties to ensure "safe passage" for its fleet.

body:
Saudi Arabia’s General Food Security Authority (GFSA) has just booked 794,000 MT of milling
wheat in an international tender at $266–$283/MT C&F, with delivery scheduled for May–July.
The award structure matters: shipments are spread across the Kingdom’s key import gateways —
Jeddah (5 cargoes), Yanbu (5), Jazan (2) and Duba (1) — a clear signal this is about system
reliability, not just price.
A sizable share is expected to be supplied from Russia and other Black Sea origins. That
expectation alone is a market message:
1) Black Sea remains the marginal price setter
Even in a world where balance sheets look comfortable, the tradable export “barrel” from
Russia/Black Sea continues to anchor global price discovery. When Saudi — one of the most
consistent importers in MENA — clears volume at this band, it effectively prints a reference level
for the region.
2) The price band is a “floor signal,” not a panic premium
$266–$283 C&F implies freight and execution risk are priced, but not a scarcity surcharge. In
plain terms: wheat is available, but the market still pays for timing, logistics and certainty.
3) Saudi procurement is steady, staged coverage
GFSA is not buying hand-to-mouth. This tender covers late spring into early summer — a period
that overlaps with Northern Hemisphere supply refresh. Also notable: GFSA’s prior tender
(January) was roughly 907,000 MT, showing a consistent cadence of replenishing strategic and
commercial pipeline needs.

title: The Great Ag-Realignment: Narrowing U.S. Trade Deficit is a Warning, Not a Win!!
excerpt: The U.S. agricultural trade landscape is undergoing its most radical transformation in decades.
body:
The U.S. agricultural trade landscape is undergoing its most radical transformation in decades.
While the headline figures for 2026 suggest a "rebalancing," the reality on the ground is a story of
structural contraction, intensifying geopolitical friction, and a deepening crisis of confidence in
the American heartland.
The Mirage of a Shrinking Deficit
On the surface, the news sounds positive: the USDA recently slashed the projected 2026 U.S.
agricultural trade deficit to $29 billion, a significant drop from the record-shattering $43.7 billion
deficit of 2025. However, this is not a victory of surging exports. Instead, the deficit is narrowing
because U.S. imports are collapsing faster than exports. Imports are forecasted to fall from a 2025
peak of $220 billion substantially, driven by domestic inflation and a pivot away from high-value
imported produce due to tariffs.
Meanwhile, U.S. exports remain stuck in a downward spiral, forecasted at $174 billion, trailing to
last year's $175 billion and significantly behind the $196 billion peak seen just a few years ago.
The China Exit and the South American Wall
The primary engine of this export slump is the aggressive decoupling from China. U.S.
agricultural exports to China are projected to crater to just $9 billion in 2026, down from $17
billion in 2025 and a fraction of the $36 billion levels reached during previous peaks. This retreat
is being met by a "wall of grain" from the south. Brazil is on track for a record-breaking 182
million tonne soybean harvest, pricing its supply nearly $1.00 per bushel below U.S. Gulf values.

a nearly 68% increase from the $74 duty applied in the previous month. When you layer on the
separate 10% export levy—calculated as a distinct charge on the reference price—the total fiscal
burden per tonne exported becomes a formidable barrier to cheap supply.
This tax strategy is a clear signal: Indonesia is prioritizing domestic price stability and the
funding of its massive "downstream" industrialization and biodiesel programs.

title: The U.S. Economy: Soft Landing Just Hit a Wall!!

body:
For the past year, the global narrative has been obsessed with the "soft landing"—the idea that
central banks could tame inflation without breaking the back of the real economy. But as we
move through March 2026, the data suggests that the landing is going to be far harder than
anticipated. We aren't just looking at a "cooling" economy; we are watching the structural pillars
of U.S. growth buckle under the weight of geopolitical reality.
The signs are no longer subtle. They are flashing red across every major dashboard.
1. The Energy Tax and the Consumer Breaking Point
History is a ruthless teacher. Nearly every U.S. recession since 1945 has been preceded by a sharp
spike in energy costs. With Brent crude entrenched between $110 and $120 per barrel due to the
escalating conflict in the Middle East, the "energy tax" on the American consumer has reached a
tipping point. When the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index falls to 55.5—a level
historically reserved for deep crises—discretionary spending doesn't just slow down; it
evaporates. In an economy where 70% of GDP is driven by consumption, a demoralized
consumer is a precursor to a contraction.
2. The Labor Market’s Signal
We have transitioned from "labor hoarding" to active job shedding. The loss of 92,000 jobs in
February 2026 wasn't just a statistical blip; it was a systemic shock. With unemployment ticking
up to 4.4%, we have effectively triggered the "Sahm Rule" logic—where a 0.5% rise in the
unemployment rate from its cycle low almost always signals the start of a recession. The cracks
are deepest in the service sector, where the PMI has plummeted to 51.7, indicating an engine that
is nearly out of fuel.

body:

have a "mountain of masoor (lentils)" building up; on the other, a domestic Indian market that
refuses to blink. If you’re looking at headline global prices and expecting a dip in Indian mandis,
you’re missing the structural "ballast" keeping our local markets steady.
The 2025–26 Masoor season is defying the gravity of a global surplus.
The Global "Mountain of Masoor"
The numbers out of the export hubs are staggering. Canada is sitting on a total production of
roughly 3.4 million tons (MMT), with red lentils alone accounting for 1.8 MMT. Combine that with
Australia’s robust forecast of 1.9 MMT, and you have a global red lentil pool of nearly 3.7 MMT.
In a vacuum, this volume would trigger a price freefall. Indeed, we see this reflected in current
global offers, with CFR India values hovering around $590 USD/MT. However, the world of
commodities doesn’t exist in a vacuum—it exists in a landscape of logistics and currency.
The Currency and Freight "Tax"
The $590 global price is effectively a mirage for the Indian importer. With the USD to INR
exchange rate sitting at 94, the currency depreciation acts as a massive hidden cost. When you
layer on the current 10% import duty the landed cost of imported lentils climbs to approximately
₹61,000 per ton.
The "Currency and War Tax" means that even as prices at the origin (Canada and Australia)
weaken due to the glut, the cost of bringing that supply to an Indian warehouse remains high
enough to provide a protective buffer for our farmers.

escalation path:

excerpt: Forget the early 2025 forecasts of a massive Brazilian sugar surplus. The theoretical food vs.
body:
Forget the early 2025 forecasts of a massive Brazilian sugar surplus. The theoretical food vs. fuel
dynamic at the mill gate has exploded into a physical reality. The highly discussed "structural
option" to flip cane allocation from sugar exports to domestic ethanol is no longer a risk—it is the
active trade flow.
The washouts have started, and they are violent.
The Perfect Storm: Rock-Bottom Sugar vs. Historic-High Ethanol
The economic calculation driving this pivot is simple and brutal. Let's look at the triggers:
Sugar’s February Collapse: Up until the end of last month, global sugar prices were at rock
bottom. On February 12, 2026, benchmark US Sugar #11 futures plummeted to 13.34 cents per
pound—the lowest price recorded since October 2020. Millions of tonnes of Brazilian export
contracts were booked at these depressed, highly reduced price levels.
Historically High Ethanol Prices: While sugar was crashing, Brazil’s internal ethanol market was
exploding. Throughout early 2026, prices have remained at historically high levels, currently up
7.4% year-over-year and rising 5.0% over just the last three months.
Severe Ethanol Scarcity: This pricing power is driven by an absolute shortage of fuel supply. As
of mid-February, Brazilian hydrous ethanol stocks were 22% lower than the same period last
year, while anhydrous stocks were down 24%.
The Policy Match in the Powder Keg
The Brazilian government just applied the final accelerant. To secure local fuel supplies, the
ethanol blend rate in gasoline was recently raised from 27% to 30%. Furthermore, Brasília is
reportedly considering a further emergency increase to 35%.

food security. While global headlines remain fixated on energy flows, the real "ground-level"

retail price of imported Wheat and Rice has surged by 25% to 35% in just the last three weeks.
These economies, built on highly efficient "just-in-time" inventory models, are now tapping into
strategic reserves faster than they can be replenished. The Quartet represents a massive, neartotal dependency, with an estimated 2026 annual import requirement of approximately 6.2

the grain complex, the mathematics of the 2026 harvest have shifted. We are no longer just

a crop.

more expensive than just last month, when prices averaged $4.40 per bushel ($173.22 per ton) in

to a high of $4.95 per bushel ($194.87 per ton). This rally is aggressively testing those upper
resistance levels as the "Middle East Premium" gets baked into the December new-crop contracts.

orchestrating a massive shift. The USDA projects corn plantings to drop by 4.8 million acres to 94

dominance as the world’s top exporter. While 2026 was heralded as the year of a great recovery
following the lifting of export curbs, it has instead transformed into a logistical and geopolitical

We are staring at a paradoxical disaster: record-breaking production colliding with a paralyzed
delivery mechanism abroad.
The numbers are alarming. Approximately 400,000 tonnes of Indian rice are currently stranded
at ports or idling at sea, unable to reach key Middle Eastern markets due to the escalating conflict
in the Gulf. For a nation that accounts for 40% of global rice trade, this is a systemic
strangulation. The "Ramadan Surge" that exporters bank on every year simply vanished. Instead,
we are seeing domestic Basmati prices saw a dip of 10% per ton already as panic sets in. The
domestic prices are falling at a far elevated pace.
The math is simple and terrifying: if these stranded consignments are diverted back into the
domestic market, we will trigger a supply glut that will crash prices, wiping out the margins for
7,000+ exporters and millions of farmers who invested heavily in this "bumper" crop.
While global FOB prices for non-basmati varieties have hit historic, decadal lows—often seen in
the $330–$350 per ton range—the "Freight Tax" is killing the trade. Sea freight costs have surged
by an additional $400 per ton. This isn't just an incremental increase; it is a de facto tariff that, in
some cases, represents nearly 100% of the grain’s value. We are effectively paying an "Insecurity
Premium" that makes Indian rice uncompetitive, even as regional rivals like Pakistan see their
volumes plummet by over 35% and Thailand’s exports to West Asia come to a grinding halt.

commercial shipping at scale has effectively abandoned the route. Any "dark" transits represent
a negligible fraction of the former volume.
Conclusion:

and hydrocarbons creates a global multiplier effect, moving the crisis from a logistics delay to a

The EPA’s emergency waiver on E15 and E10 fuel standards isn't an environmental policy.
Allowing nationwide year-round sales of E15 (15% ethanol blend) starting May 1, 2026 in the
hyper-volatile landscape is a surgical economic strike against $120 oil. By waiving the traditional
seasonal restrictions on higher ethanol blends, the administration is effectively activating a
"Domestic Strategic Petroleum Reserve" buried in the cornfields of the American Midwest.
This regulatory shift is a vital shield for global crude prices under fire due to energy supply chain
disruption.
The Crude Math: Saving a Million Barrels a Day
In a "Best-Case Scenario," the logic is purely volumetric. The U.S. consumes approximately 375
million gallons of gasoline per day. Moving from a standard 10% blend (E10) to a 15% blend (E15)
across the national infrastructure replaces an additional 5% of every gallon of petroleum with
bio-based ethanol.
The impact is staggering when scaled. That 5% shift represents roughly 18.75 million gallons of
petroleum saved every single day. When you convert that into crude oil equivalents—accounting
for refinery yields where one barrel of crude produces about 19 to 20 gallons of gasoline—this
policy has the potential to reduce U.S. crude demand by nearly 950,000 to 1,000,000 barrels per
day.
In an era where global production has plummeted and strategic reserves are being burned at
record rates, finding an "internal well" that produces a million barrels a day without drilling a
single new hole is the ultimate economic arbitrage.

The core of the July 2025 framework—the Turnberry Deal—was a 15% tariff ceiling on most EU

Political shocks — from a US Supreme Court ruling on tariff powers to renewed Greenland-linked
threats — reinforced Brussels’ shift from simple ratification to a multi-tier safety net.
EU lawmakers have introduced specific "tripwires" that every stakeholder must now factor into
their 2027-2028 projections:
The Sunrise Clause: Tariff preferences for US goods will only switch on once US has actually
reduced its tariffs on EU products with steel and aluminium content below 50% to a maximum of
15%, with additional snapbacks for high-content products. This is a "Proof of Performance" trade
policy.
The Suspension Trigger: The EU has reserved the right to pause the deal instantly if the US slaps
new politically-motivated tariffs on the EU or a member state, or if US imports surge in a way
that harms EU industry.

plant never has a "good day"; it only has days where it loses less yield potential than others.
Reaching 6.72 MT/Ha requires:
Constant Surveillance: Tissue sampling up to three times a week to identify nutrient "hunger"
before visual symptoms appear.
Spoon-Feeding: Precision application of macro and micronutrients through high-clearance rigs
or irrigation, tailored to the specific growth stage.
Uniformity as Law: Ensuring every seed is placed at the exact same depth and spacing to prevent
"neighbor competition."

body:
The USDA’s Prospective Plantings report, released March 31, 2026, confirms a significant
structural shift in the American heartland. Faced with a "Reverse Black Swan" in energy and
input costs, U.S. producers are aggressively rotating away from high-input "nutrient-heavy"
staples in favor of soybeans and cotton. Overall acreage is expected to fall to 309.95 million acres
(MAc) from last year's 311.54 MAc
The Corn Retreat: A 3.45 Million Acre Void
The most visible casualty of rising input sensitivity is corn. Intended area for all purposes is
estimated at 95.3 MAc, a 3% decline from 2025.
Systemic Decline: Planted acreage is expected to be down or unchanged in 37 of 48 estimating
states.
The "I" States Pull Back: Major production hubs are leading the retreat, with decreases of
300,000+ acres projected in Illinois, Iowa, Minnesota, Nebraska, North Dakota, South Dakota, and
Wisconsin.
Nitrogen Pressure: This comes despite historic production highs, as farmers prioritize the lower
nitrogen requirements of legumes over the intensive fertilization needs of corn.
The Soybean Surge: Nitrogen-Fixing Resilience
Conversely, soybeans are the primary beneficiary of the acreage pivot. Estimated at 84.7 MAc,
soybean intentions are up 4% year-over-year.
Widespread Adoption: Intentions are up or unchanged in 20 of 29 estimating states.
Record Growth: Wisconsin is on track for a record high soybean acreage.

body:
The global vegetable oil complex is witnessing a "perfect rally" as Crude Palm Oil (CPO) futures
hit 4,850 MYR/TNE. The velocity of this rally is staggering: a 1.59% jump in a single day and a
5.49% rise over the last five days. More tellingly, we’ve seen a massive 16.94% surge in just one
month, contributing to a year-to-date gain of 20.55%. When viewed over a longer horizon, the
price is up 8.78% over six months and 7.05% over the last year, signaling that the market has
officially shattered previous resistance levels to enter a structural bull phase.
The Architecture of a Rally
This isn't mere speculation; it is driven by a convergence of four unyielding factors:

1️⃣ The Indonesian B50 Mandate: Indonesia’s aggressive pivot toward a 50% biodiesel blend is
effectively removing couple of millions of tonnes from the global exportable surplus. By

prioritizing domestic energy security, the world's largest producer is tightening the "free float" of
palm oil, forcing international buyers to scramble.

2️⃣ The Energy Floor: With WTI and Brent Crude holding firm north of $100/bbl, palm oil’s role as
a biofuel feedstock is locked in. High fossil fuel prices provide a hard floor for vegetable oils,
making "discretionary blending" highly profitable and keeping CPO in high demand.

3️⃣ Production Hurdles: Southeast Asia is grappling with the cumulative effect of aging

plantations and delayed replanting cycles. Lower oil extraction rates and stagnant yields mean
that even as prices rise, the physical supply cannot be "turned on" overnight.

4️⃣ The Logistics Advantage: For large consumers like India and China, palm oil remains the

pragmatic choice. While South American soyoil is a substitute, the higher maritime insurance
and fuel surcharges for Atlantic routes make Malaysian and Indonesian CPO significantly more
attractive on a landed-cost (CIF) basis.

title: Rhetoric vs. Reality: The Final Verdict on Liberation Day Tariffs!!

body:
One year ago, the announcement of "Liberation Day" tariffs was presented as a pivotal moment
for American trade—a "rectification" of long-standing imbalances and a catalyst for a domestic
manufacturing renaissance.
However, as we mark this anniversary in April 2026, a cold analysis of the data reveals a starkly
different reality. Far from the promised boom, the empirical evidence suggests that the policy
has not only failed to achieve its primary objectives but has introduced new structural
vulnerabilities into the U.S. and global economies.
The Trade Deficit Paradox
The fundamental goal of the Liberation Day tariffs was to narrow the goods trade deficit by
penalizing imports and encouraging domestic substitution. Yet, the Bureau of Economic Analysis
(BEA) reported that the U.S. goods deficit actually touched an all-time high in 2025.
This outcome is a textbook example of how tariffs, when applied broadly as a blunt instrument,
can backfire.
The Manufacturing "Boom" That Never Arrived
Perhaps the most significant promise of the 2025 policy was the revitalization of U.S.
manufacturing capacity. The actual data, however, paints a picture of contraction rather than
expansion:
Job Losses: In 2025 the U.S. manufacturing sector shed approximately 100,000 jobs.
Employment Ratios: The ratio of manufacturing workers to total nonfarm employment has fallen
to its lowest point since 1939.

🚨 *2026 Monsoon Alert: The El Niño Threat to Indian Agriculture* 🚨
The global climate cycle is shifting, and the implications for Indian agriculture are severe. The
latest April 2026 NOAA confirms that the Pacific is transitioning from a weak La Niña to ENSOneutral, with a high probability of a powerful El Niño developing during the monsoon. The
projected outcome? A subpar Indian Southwest Monsoon at 94% of the Long Period Average
(LPA).
While a delicately positive Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) might offer slight initial buffering, El Niño
will dominate the second half of the season.
Agriculture must prepare for prolonged breaks, intense heatwaves, and heightened drought risks
across central, western, and northwestern India—paradoxically coupled with extreme daily
rainfall bursts in core zones like the Western Ghats and central-eastern India.

title: RBI’s Rupee Defence: A Tactical Victory, But at What Strategic Cost? 🇮🇳
excerpt:
As the global economy braces for compounding macro-shocks from the West Asian conflict and
surging energy markets, the Reserve Bank of India is fighting a fierce battle to steady the Rupee. But the recent policy pivot reveals uncomfortable trade-offs between tactical market control and
long-term economic confidence.
body:
As the global economy braces for compounding macro-shocks from the West Asian conflict and
surging energy markets, the Reserve Bank of India is fighting a fierce battle to steady the Rupee.
But the recent policy pivot reveals uncomfortable trade-offs between tactical market control and
long-term economic confidence.
In late March 2026, to halt a rapid, panic-driven slide past ₹95/USD, the RBI abruptly capped
banks’ net open rupee positions at $100 million (effective April 10) and aggressively tightened
access to non-deliverable forwards (NDFs).
The immediate result was a textbook short squeeze. As banks forcefully unwound tens of billions
in positions, dollar supply flooded the market. The INR roared back with a >2% single-day rally—
its sharpest rebound since 2013—finding temporary stability around the ₹92.6–92.7 mark.
However, viewing this purely through the lens of near-term stabilization misses the broader
macroeconomic fallout.

📉 1. Exacerbating the Capital Account Deficit (CAD) Risk

India structurally runs a Current Account Deficit. The inherent gravitational pull on the Rupee is
downward unless that gap is funded by robust foreign capital inflows. By deploying opaque,
restrictive measures with limited advance guidance, the RBI has spiked policy unpredictability.
We are already seeing foreign investors trim Indian bond exposures in response to perceived
higher risk. If these frictions deter the vital FDI and FPI needed to fund the CAD, the central bank
paradoxically amplifies the long-term structural weakness of the very currency it is trying to
protect.

body:
The global agricultural supply chain is currently facing a highly specialized, unprecedented
crisis. It is not just about natural gas, urea, amonia or potash; right now, the world is rapidly
running out of accessible sulfur. This severe shortage of sulfuric acid—a critical chemical
precursor required to process raw phosphate rock—is forcing major global fertilizer producers
to scale back operations, idle plants, and absorb massive margin compression.
The sulfur deficit is paralyzing the industry's largest players.

🌎 The Global Giants Under Pressure:
🇧🇷 The Mosaic Company (Brazil/USA): Mosaic is severely exposed. They have been forced to idle
single super phosphate (SSP) production at their Fospar and Araxá facilities in Brazil. To avoid

ruinous, record-high spot prices, they have completely suspended open-market sulfur purchases,
relying solely on dwindling long-term contracts.

🇲🇦 OCP Group (Morocco): The absolute titan of global phosphates is feeling the pinch. OCP is

advancing scheduled maintenance across several plants, effectively stripping up to 30% of its
production capacity in Q2 2026 to manage the exceptionally tight sulfur and ammonia supply.

🇷🇺 PhosAgro (Russia): Facing severe internal sulfur shortages as Gazprom's technical sulfur

production (at Astrakhan and Orenburg) plummets. PhosAgro has been pushed to request a
temporary price cap as margins for non-integrated DAP fertilizers have plunged into negative
territory.

title: The Ethanol Illusion: Is the Sunshine Era of India’s Biofuel Dream Over?
excerpt:

body:
Just 24 months ago, Indian ethanol was the undisputed darling of the agro-industrial sector.
Propelled by aggressive E20 blending mandates, capital flooded into the ecosystem, creating a
gold rush of massive new capacities.
Today, and the narrative has violently flipped. We are witnessing shrinking plant utilization,
severely compressed margins, and a looming existential crisis for operators.
Beyond the green-energy rhetoric the raw, structural realities are breaking the sector's back.

🏭 1. The Utilization Collapse & Inefficient Productivity

We built for a boom but are operating in a bust. Nationwide distillery capacity has surged, yet
actual plant utilization is plummeting to dangerous lows. Facilities designed for 300+ days of
continuous operation are struggling to maintain consistent runs. In a capital-intensive industry,
high fixed costs against low volumetric output is a fatal financial combination. The productivity
of these assets is tanking, making debt servicing a nightmare.

🌾 2. The Feedstock Whiplash

The fundamental flaw of the Indian ethanol model is its acute vulnerability to food inflation
panic. When sugar prices threatened to spike, policy justifiably clamped down on using
sugarcane juice and B-heavy molasses to protect consumer affordability.
